journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Nutrition, Health & Aging

title-group.article-title: The Effects of Concurrent Training Order on Satellite Cell-Related Markers, Body Composition, Muscular and Cardiorespiratory Fitness in Older Men with Sarcopenia

contrib-group.aff: 1Department of Exercise Physiology, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad, Iran
2Department of Exercise Physiology, University of Isfahan, Isfahan, Iran
3Department of Clinical Biochemistry, School of Medicine, Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences, Ahvaz, Iran
4Department of Kinesiology & Sport Management, Texas Tech University, 79424, Lubbock, TX, USA
5Department of Exercise Physiology, University of Birjand, Birjand, Iran
6Centre for Health and Exercise Science Research, Department of Sport, Physical Education and Health, Hong Kong Baptist University, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong
7Exercise & Sport Nutrition Lab, Human Clinical Research Facility, Department of Health & Kinesiology, Texas A&M University, 77843-4243, College Station, TX, USA
8Department of Health and Human Performance, Marymount University, Arlington, USA
9Faculty of Social Sciences, Centre for Health and Exercise Science Research, Department of Sport, Physical Education and Health, Hong Kong Baptist University, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: Concurrent Training (CT) is described as a combination of resistance training (RT) and endurance training (ET) in a periodized program to maximize all aspects of physical performance. To date, effects of CT order on muscular and cardiorespiratory fitness adaptations are controversial. Owing to the age-related decrement in satellite cells (SC) which are critical for fiber repair, conservation, muscle hypertrophy as well as cardiorespiratory fitness, the present study examined the response of SC related markers to CT order in older sarcopenic men.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Thirty older men (age= 64.3 ± 3.5 years) were randomly assigned into one of 3 groups, ET followed by RT (E+R; n=10), RT followed by ET (R+E; n= 10) or a control (C; n=10).

sec.title: Intervention
sec.p: The training protocol consisted of 3 exercise sessions per week for 8 weeks. Blood samples were obtained at baseline and 48 hours after the final training session.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Weight, skeletal muscle mass, lower and upper body power, maximal oxygen consumption (VO2max), Paired Box 7 (Pax7), and Myogenic factor 5 (Myf5) significantly increased, while were percent body fat significantly decreased following E+R and R+E compared to C. Importantly, the improvement in skeletal muscle mass, lower and upper body power, Myf5 and Pax7 in the E+R was significantly greater than the R+E group. Myogenin (Myog) and Paired Box 3 (Pax3) significantly increased (P < 0.01) in both training groups compared to no changes in C.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: An 8-week CT intervention improves SC related markers, body composition and enhances power and VO2max in older sarcopenic participants, regardless of the order of RT and ET. However, performing ET before RT may be more effective at enhancing skeletal muscle mass, Myf5 and Pax7, in addition to both lower and upper body power. While both CT programs produced notable physiological and performance benefits, performing ET before RT during CT may provide the greatest therapeutic benefits for aging individuals.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Sarcopenia is a condition that is associated with aging and characterized by a degenerative loss of skeletal muscle mass, strength, power, and cardiorespiratory fitness (1, 2). A decline in the number of both Type I and II muscle fibers accompanies sarcopenia, with preferential atrophy in Type II muscle fibers (3, 4). Satellite cells (SC) are located inside skeletal muscle fibers and are critical for fiber repair, conservation and muscle hypertrophy (5). Maintenance of the SC pool is essential in maintaining muscle health throughout adulthood (6). Previous research has demonstrated a lower content of SC in older adults when compared to young individuals, as indicated by transcription factors (e.g. Pax3 and Pax7) and myogenic regulatory factors (MRFs; e.g. Myf5, MyoD, Myog, and MRF4) (5, 7, 8). The age-related loss of SC content and function might have a significant role in the sarcopenic process since SC are responsible for inducing the fusion of myoblasts with myotubes or myoblast differentiation to form new myonuclei (9).

Resistance training (RT) has been reported as an efficacious interventional approach to contest age-related muscular decrements (2). Indeed, numerous studies have reported the beneficial influence of RT training on SC content as well as SC related proteins (10, 11, 12). However, RT has little or no effect on maximum rate of oxygen consumption (VO2max) (13), an indicator of cardiorespiratory fitness strongly associated with cardiovascular risk (14). Therefore, it is optimal for older individuals to perform RT with other training modalities to increase VO2max, including endurance training (ET) in order to improve both muscular health and cardiorespiratory fitness (2).

Since adaptations to RT versus ET are generally divergent and may be affected by various factors such as modality and participant's characteristics, it seems necessary for older individuals to concurrently perform both training modalities to maximize health. Indeed, incorporating RT and ET simultaneously in the same training period, known as concurrent training (CT), has been shown to enhance physiological capability (15). However, although CT can produce beneficial adaptations, there are concerns about whether RT or ET adaptations could be compromised due to the presence of both exercise types within an overall training program. This concept termed the “interference effect phenomenon,” which has been supported by previous research. Specifically, several studies reported compromised muscular strength and hypertrophy adaptations to training in response to concurrently performing ET within an overall training program (16, 17). However, other studies have indicated that these detrimental effects are modulated by the frequency and duration of the ET sessions and that interference can be appropriately managed with prudent program design (18).

Limited data have been presented regarding the influence of within-session exercise order on interference between acute RT and ET performance or chronic adaptations. However, a speculative rationale for either CT order can be provided when considering possible interference effects between the signaling pathways involved in adaptations to RT and ET (i.e., molecular interference) (16). From a practical perspective, the fatigue and substrate depletion of ET could compromise subsequent RT performance, theoretically supporting a CT order of RT followed by ET. Conversely, based on the time course of metabolic signaling events, it could also be suggested that performing ET prior to RT within a given session may ultimately reduce detrimental interactions between divergent molecular pathways. Based on the transient activation of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) after ET, relative to the longer-lasting mechanistic target of rapamycin (mTOR)-based responses after RT, performing RT after ET could potentially minimize the detriments of ET on RT signaling and responses (16). In contrast to this molecular interference-based rationale, a prior investigation in elderly men has indicated that performing RT prior to ET may lead to enhanced skeletal muscle quality (i.e., force production per unit of muscle thickness) and lower-body strength gains (19). While the definitive occurrence of molecular interference based on CT order is yet to be established, and the translation between acute findings and long-term adaptations is uncertain, the paucity of data indicate that additional investigation is warranted.

Regarding exercise intervention studies investigating SC, it has been previously shown that 12 weeks of RT leads to an increase in type II muscle fiber SC content in elderly men (11). In addition, combining RT in the upper limbs with ET in the lower limbs have shown an efficient strategy to enhance the SC content in upper and lower body muscles in elderly participants (20). Furthermore, in a recent study, the authors reported that performing prolonged exercise training improves the acute type II muscle fiber SC response following a single bout of RT in older men (21). Interestingly, it has also been demonstrated that low load RT in sarcopenic older men is associated with a higher muscle cross sectional area (CSA), increased SC content, and capillarization (22). Combining high-intensity interval training after a single bout of RT does not interfere with the increase in type-I-specific total, and possibly active, SC content, compared to RT only in sedentary, overweight/obese, middle-aged individuals (23). However, concurrent low volume RT (10 minutes) and high volume EE (90 minutes), compromised the SC response to acute RT (24).

To the best of our knowledge, no investigations have examined the effects of CT order on plasma SC related markers. In addition, prior studies have investigated the acute response of an exercise session on SC. Therefore, the present study was implemented to determine whether the order of RT and ET within a CT program would affect physiological adaptations to chronic training, particularly the response of SC related markers. We hypothesized that CT, regardless of RT and ET order, would increase SC related markers and improve physical performance in older men with sarcopenia.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Thirty older men participated in the study. Inclusion criteria for subject acceptance into the study were: aged between 60 and 70 years, presence of sarcopenia (diagnosed based on handgrip strength values of less than 26–30 kg, walking speed of less than 0.8 m/s over 4 meters, and a skeletal muscle mass index of less than two standard deviations below the average young adult population) , sedentary (defined as less than 1 hour of exercise per week performed in the previous year) and free of major diseases (Parkinson's disease, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, etc.) (2). Further criteria included being a non-smoker, not participating in hormonal or mental therapy, not engaged in regular moderate to heavy training within the past year, as well as not consuming alcohol, no dietary supplementation and non-usage of certain drugs (i.e. those influencing muscle metabolisms). Inclusion criteria were assessed by a physician using the Physical Activity Readiness-Questionnaire (PAR-Q) and a medical health/history questionnaire. The study protocol was approved by the Institutional Human Subject ethics committee and was performed in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p:
A schematic of the study design is presented in Figure 1. Prior to baseline measurements, participants were fully familiarized with experimental procedures and were randomly assigned to into one of three groups: ET followed by RT (E+R; n=10), RT followed by ET (R+E; n= 10) or a non-exercise control group (C; n=10). Measurements were conducted at baseline and repeated following the 8-week intervention (48–72 hours after the last training session). All assessments were conducted at the same time of day, within ∼1 hour, and under the same environmental conditions (∼20°C and ∼55% humidity). Participants were instructed not to alter their regular lifestyle and dietary habits throughout the study.Figure 1
Schematic of the study design. Both baseline and post-testing were conducted between 8 and 8:30 AM after a 12-hour overnight fast and avoidance of exercise. 3-day food diaries were recorded before and at the end of the study in all groups

Abbreviations: E+R, endurance training + resistance training; R+E, endurance training + resistance training; C, control; BW, body weight; H, height; SMM, skeletal muscle mass: PBF, percent body fat; UB, upper body; LB, lower body; Myf5, Myogenic factor 5; Myog, myogenin; Pax7, Paired Box 7; Pax3, Paired Box 3.

sec.title: Anthropometry and Body Composition
sec.p: Participants were asked to urinate (void) completely within 30 min prior to reporting to the lab. Upon entering the laboratory, body weight measurements to the nearest 0.1 kg using a calibrated digital scale were obtained (Lumbar, China). Height was measured to the nearest 0.1 cm using a stadiometer (Race industrialization, China). Body mass index (BMI), percent body fat, and skeletal muscle mass were evaluated using a multi-frequency bioelectrical impedance device (Inbody 720, South Korea). Participants were instructed to complete an overnight fast of 12 hours (25), attempt to sleep for at least 8 hours and refrain from physical activity for 48 hours prior to the test.

sec.title: Performance Testing
sec.p: Strength testing took place 24 hours following body composition assessments and blood sampling. Prior to testing, research personnel explained the purpose of each test, associated risks, possible discomforts and the responsibilities of participants. All participants were instructed to refrain from caffeinated drinks for 12 hours, and food intake for 2 hours prior to the testing session; however, ad libitum water consumption was allowed.

Following a light warm-up, using both general and specific exercises, the participants performed strength testing protocols for all the exercises used in the RT methodology (i.e. leg extension, leg curl, bench press, lat pulldown, lateral raise, abdominal machine crunch). The highest lifted weight and number of repetitions to fatigue were recorded for each subject. The number of repetitions to fatigue did not exceed ten. There was a 5 min rest period between attempts. The participants performed two attempts and their highest lifted weight and number of repetitions were recorded. The one-repetition maximum (1RM) was estimated using a prediction formula based on the number of repetitions performed to volitional fatigue using a given weight. 1RM= weight/ (1.0278–0.0278×reps) (26).

For power testing, the 30-second Wingate test on a cycle ergometer (Monark, 894E) was used to measure upper and lower body maximal power as previously described (27, 28). For cardiorespiratory fitness assessments, the Modified Bruce protocol was used to determine VO2max as previously described (29).

sec.title: Concurrent Training
sec.p: Participants in the E+R and R+E groups completed supervised training 3 times a week for 8 weeks, with sessions separated by at least 48 hours. The E+R group performed ET first, followed by RT; while the R+E group performed RT first, and followed by ET.

sec.title: Preparatory Phase
sec.p: Before the beginning of the intervention, participants in the E+R and R+E groups performed 1 week of concurrent training, consisting of three exercise sessions, for familiarization purposes. This phase allowed for the instruction of correct lifting technique and familiarization with all exercises and equipment (30).

sec.title: RT program
sec.p:
Following the preparatory phase, RT began with 2 sets of 16 to 18 repetitions at 40% of 1RM in the first week and progressed to 3 sets of 8 to 10 repetitions at 75% 1RM in the eighth week. Two-minute rest periods were employed throughout the study. The specific exercises included in the program were leg extension, leg curl, bench press, lat pulldown, lateral raise, and abdominal crunch (Table 1). The periodized protocols were adapted from a previous study that investigated older sarcopenic individuals (31, 32) and followed recommendations of the National Strength and Conditioning Association (33). All exercises were performed on variable resistance machines (Technogym equipment, Italy).Table 1
Resistance and endurance exercise protocol
WeekWeekly training sessionsResistance exerciseEndurance exercise
SetsRest interval (min)RepetitionsIntensity (%1RM)Volume (minutes)Intensity (%HRmax)RPE (Borg scale)
132214–1640155511
232214–1645155511
332212–1450206013
432212–1455206013
533310–1260256515
633310–1265256515
73338–1070307017
83338–1075307017

sec.title: ET program
sec.p: The ET program consisted of cycling on a fixed-speed cycle ergometer. Sessions began at an intensity of 55% of maximum heart rate for 15 minutes in the first week and progressed to 70% of maximum heart rate for 30 minutes in the last week (Table 1). A heart rate monitor (Polar S810, Polar Electro, Kempele, Finland) was used to control and measure the exercise intensities. The periodized ET protocol was adapted from previous studies on older sarcopenic individuals (34, 35) and followed recommendations by the National Strength and Conditioning Association (33).

sec.title: Blood Sampling and Analysis
sec.p: Prior to each blood draw, participants were instructed to complete an overnight fast for 12 hours and refrain from physical activity for the previous 36 hours. Blood samples (5 mL) were obtained from the cubital vein using standard procedures 48 hours prior to the baseline training session. Blood samples were collected into Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) anticoagulant tubes and immediately centrifuged (3000 rpm for 15 minutes). The plasma was used to measure the concentration of the SC related markers Myogenic factor 5 (Myf5), myogenin (Myog), Paired Box 7 (Pax7), and Paired Box 3 (Pax3). The following values were obtained for accuracy and reliability: Plasma Myf5 (sensitivity of 0.02 ng / ml, intraassay CV <8% and interassay CV <10%), Myog (sensitivity < 0.375 ng / ml, intraassay CV < 8% and interassay CV <10%), Pax7 (sensitivity of < 5.8 pg / ml, intraassay CV < 8%, and interassay CV < 10%), and Pax3 (sensitivity of 0.1 ng / ml, intraassay CV < 15%, and interassay CV < 15%). All concentrations were measured by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (MyBioSource, USA).

sec.title: Nutrient intake and dietary analysis
sec.p: Because of the importance of nutritional timing during and around the exercise sessions, dietary intake in the hours near (before and after) the training sessions were controlled. The participants consumed a banana, providing ∼0.30–0.35 g of carbohydrate per kilogram of body weight, as a pre-exercise snack ∼1 hour before the training session. Additionally, dinner was consumed ∼1.5–2 hours after each training session and was standardized to contain 1.7 g/kg of body weight of carbohydrate, 0.3 g/kg of body weight of protein, 0.4g/kg of body weight of fat (36). These recommendations were based on the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics, Dietitians of Canada and the American College of Sports Medicine recommendations for macronutrient distribution (55–65% of total calories from carbohydrates, <35% of total calories from fats and 10–15% of total calories from protein). Protein timing was also considered and implemented during the study period, with ∼0.25 to 0.3 g of protein/kg of body weight consumed within 2 hours after each exercise session (37). Beyond this peri-exercise nutrient intake standardization, the participants were instructed not to alter their dietary habits during the study. Participants were required to submit 3-day (2 weekdays and 1 weekend) food records at baseline and near the completion of the assigned intervention. Each food item was individually entered into the Diet Analysis Plus version 10 (Cengage, Boston, MA, USA) program and total energy consumption, as well as the amount of energy derived from proteins, fats, and carbohydrates, was assessed.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The normality of the data from the study was confirmed using the Shapiro-Wilk test. Based on earlier investigations, it was established that 10 participants per group would provide 80% power (2-sided α= 0.05) to detect 9%, 8% and 7% changes in Myog, Myf5, and Pax7, respectively [38–40]. Possible group differences at baseline were examined using a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). A time (baseline vs. 8 weeks) × group (E+R vs. R+E vs. C) factorial ANOVA with repeated measures was performed to determine differences between the treatments. This was followed by the appropriate Scheffeâ€™s post hoc test when a significant treatment and treatment-by-time interaction was revealed. Statistical significance was set at P < 0.05. IBM SPSS (version 24, IBM) was used to analyze the data.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p:
Participants in the E+R and R+E groups completed 100% of exercise sessions. There were no significant differences in participant characteristics at baseline (Table 2). There were no significant differences in total energy consumption, and the amount of energy derived from proteins, fats, and carbohydrates at baseline (Table 3). There were no significant changes after 8 weeks for dietary intake.Table 2
Participant's characteristics of at baseline and after 8 weeks
VariablesR+EE+RC
BaselineEndBaselineEndBaselineEnd
Age (years)63.8 ± 3.6-----64.1 ± 3.3-----65 ± 3.9-----
Height (cm)166 ± 3.8-----165.2 ± 4.2-----163.7 ± 3.4-----
Weight (kg)62.8 ± 2.363.8 ± 2.361.1 ± 3.163.5 ± 2.661.4 ± 4.661.2 ± 4.7
BMI (kg/m2)22.8 ± 1.023.1 ± 0.922.4 ± 1.223.3 ± 1.122.9 ± 1.322.8 ± 1.3
Data are mean ± SD. Abbreviations: RT, resistance training; ET, endurance training, C; Control. BMI, body mass index.Table 3
Energy and macronutrients at baseline after 8 weeks
GroupBaseline8 weeksp-value
Energy (kcal/d)R+E1989.5 ± 160.51985.3 ± 176.30.8
E+R1993.6 ± 2441987.8 ± 162.90.8
C1976.6 ± 434.71967.4 ± 254.10.7
Protein (g/d)R+E51.7 ± 7.251.4 ± 7.40.9
E+R52.3 ± 13.851.8 ± 6.30.8

C49.8 ± 16.850 ± 6.50.9

E+R331.3 ± 32.4330 ± 31.00.9

Abbreviations: CHO, carbohydrate; R+E, group performing resistance exercise followed by endurance exercise; E+R, group performing endurance exercise followed by resistance exercise, C; non-exercise control group. Values are Mean ± SD.

sec.title: Body composition and fitness parameters
sec.p:
Significant main effects of time were observed for weight (p ≤ 0.01, η2 = 0.77), percent body fat (Figure 2A, p ≤ 0.05, η2 = 0.41), skeletal muscle mass (Figure 2B, p ≤ 0.01, η2 = 0.0.21), upper body power (Figure 2C, p ≤ 0.01, η2 = 0.63), lower body power (Figure 2D, p ≤ 0.01, η2 = 0.75), and VO2max (Figure 2E, p ≤ 0.01, η2 = 0.63).Figure 2
PBF, percent body fat (A); SMM, skeletal muscle mass (B); UB Power, upper body Power (C); LB Power, lower body power (D) and VO2max, maximum rate of oxygen consumption (E) at pre and post-intervention
*p<0.05 different from baseline. # P< 0.05 between group differences

There were significant group-by-time interactions (P < 0.01) for weight [E+R= 2.4 kg (95% confidence interval {CI}= 1.9 to 2.9, d= −0.83) and R+E= 1.1 kg (95% CI= 0.7 to 1.3, d= −0.43)], BMI [E+R= 0.8 kg/m2 (95% CI= 0.6 to 1, d= 0.78) and R+E= 0.3 kg/m2 (95% CI= 0.2 to 0.5, d= −0.32)], and skeletal muscle mass [E+R= 0.7 kg (95% CI= 0.1 to 0.8, d= −0.57) and R+E= 0.5 kg (95% CI= 0.2 to 0.6, d= −0.36)], which significantly increased (P < 0.01), as well as PBF [E+R= −1.5 % (95% CI= −1.7 to −1.1, d= 1.1) and R+E = −2 % (95% CI= −3.6 to −0.2, d= 1.28)], which significantly declined (P < 0.05), following E+R and R+E compared to C. Importantly, the enhancement in skeletal muscle mass in the E+R was significantly greater than the R+E group.

Among the fitness parameters analyzed, significant increases (P < 0.01) were observed in VO2max after E+R and R+E in comparison to C [E+R= 1.7 ml.kg.min (95% CI= 1 to 2.4, d= −1.7) and R+E= 2 ml.kg.min (95% CI= 1.4 to 2.5, d= −2.4)]. There were significant group-by-time interactions for lower body power [E+R= 40.4 watts (95% CI= 29.4 to 51.8, d= −2.5) and R+E= 18.4 watts (95% CI= 12.3 to 24.6, d= −2.4)] and upper-body power [E+R= 42.4 watts (95% CI= 28.1 to 56.7, d= −2.1) and R+E= 13.5 watts (95% CI= 8.4 to 18.7, d= −1.8)], which significantly (P < 0.05) increased in the E+R when compared to R+E and control. The improvement in lower and upper-body power following R+E was significant (P < 0.05) when compared to C.

sec.title: SC related markers
sec.p:
Significant main effects of time were observed for Myog (Figure 3A, p ≤ 0.05, η2 = 0.88), Pax3 (Figure 3B, p ≤ 0.05, η2 = 0.71), Pax7 (Figure 3C, p ≤ 0.05, η2 = 0.81), and Myf5 (Figure 3D, p ≤ 0.05, η2 = 0.54).Figure 3
Myog, myogenin (A); Pax3, Paired Box 3 (B) and Pax7, Paired Box 7 (C) and Myf5, Myogenic factor 5 (D) at pre and post-intervention
*p<0.05 different from baseline. # P< 0.05 between group differences.

There were significant group-by-time interactions (P < 0.01) for Myog [E+R = 0.8 ng.ml (95% CI= 0.7 to 0.9, d= −5.8) and R+E= 0.3 ng.ml (95% CI= 0.2 to 0.5, d= −2.5)] and Pax3 [E+R = 0.3 ng.ml (95% CI= 0.2 to 0.4, d= −3.5) and R+E= 0.1 ng.ml (95% CI= 0.05 to 0.2, d= −1.1)], which significantly increased (P < 0.01) in both training groups compared to no changes in C. Pax7 [E+R = 69.9 pg.ml (95% CI= 58.2 to 81.5, d= −4.2) and R+E= 18.5 pg.ml (95% CI, 10.8 to 26.3, d= −1.7)] also significantly (P < 0.01) increased in both training groups, but this rise was significantly greater (P < 0.01, group-by-time interactions) in the in the E+R when compared to R+E. Furthermore, a significant group-by-time interaction (P < 0.01) was present for Myf5 [E+R = 0.4 ng.ml (95% CI= 0.3 to 0.5, d= −4.7) and R+E= 0.2 ng.ml (95% CI= 0 to 0.5, d= −0.7)], with follow up analysis indicating an increase in the E+R group when compared to C, with no differences between R+E and any other group. However, a significant time-effect was detected for Myf5 in the R+E group.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study aimed to investigate the effects of CT order and responses of SC related markers, body composition, and fitness parameters in older men with sarcopenia. Our findings indicate that CT, regardless of ET and RT order, improves SC related markers, body composition, power and VO2max in older participants with sarcopenia; however, performing ET prior to RT may be more effective at improving Pax7, as well as lower and upper body power.

SC investigations have increased recently to assess their apparent role in muscle growth (41). Although there have been experiments conducted to determine the effect of RT and ET on SC, the present experiment is the first study to assess SC related marker responses to CT with different exercise orders. Myogenic regulatory factors (MRFs) are a group of helix-loop-helix transcription factors that lead to the commitment of SC to myogenic lineage (42). Myog and Myf5 are two types of MRFs transcription factors known to regulate myoblast proliferation and regulate terminal differentiation respectively (43). The expression of MRFs is regulated by transcription of Pax3 and Pax7. Pax3 and Pax7 plays a key role in maintaining the proliferation of progenitors and preventing early differentiation (44). Based on our results, there are significant increases for all examined MRFs and Paxs following CT, independent of the training order. However, the magnitude of the increase in Pax7 was greater in the E+R group. A limited amount of studies has been conducted to determine MRFs and Pax complex responses to exercise in older adults. Verney et al. investigated the effects of 14 weeks of CT consisting of lower body ET and upper body RT, three days per week, on the SC pool of the vastus lateralis and deltoid muscles in active elderly men.

They found that combining RT in the upper limbs with ET in the lower limbs is an efficient strategy to enhance the SC pool in elderly participants (20). Kosek et al. evaluated the acute and chronic effects of RT, performed three days per week for 16 weeks, in older sarcopenic compared to young adults (39). At the transcription level, young adults responded with greater increases in Myog and Myf5 both acutely and chronically. The acute response to Myog and Myf5 was not significant among older adults, but there were improvements in both these SC markers after 16 weeks (39). Furthermore, Kim et al reported increases in Myog but not Myf5 mRNA levels 24 hours after acute RT in older individuals with sarcopenia (38). It has been noted that the intensity of ET, rather than duration, plays a fundamental role in the expansion of the SC pool (45). In this context, SC seems to play a role in the muscular response to ET, which leads to muscle stress without significant muscle hypertrophy (45). Nederveen et al. examined the acute response of SC content following high-intensity or moderate-intensity interval training in older men, observing a significant increase in SC content, as demonstrated by elevated expression of Pax7 cells, which are associated exclusively with type I fibers, 24 and 48 hours following ET (40). Furthermore, an important association between cardiorespiratory fitness and muscle SC expansion has been previously reported in healthy participants (45). A positive relationship was found between the amount of Pax7 cells following exercise suggesting that improved VO2max following ET stimulates SC expansion (46).

Although the benefits of ET and RT alone on body composition are well documented (47), research examining CT in older populations is limited. However, evidence from a meta-analysis suggests that CT is the most efficacious means to reduce percent body fat (47). Based on our results, both CT interventions resulted in declines in percent body fat. Previous studies in elderly individuals have also reported that equivalent CT-mediated decreases in percent body fat are achieved irrespective of ET and RT order (19, 48, 49). In contrast, Sheikholeslami-Vatani er al. found a significant decrease in percent body fat when ET is performed after RT following 8 weeks of CT in obese young men (50). The discrepancy between these observations may be related to methodological differences between studies or variations in participant characteristics, such as body composition or age. There is a different response in older than young individuals, which may be related to age-related changes in SC activation due to differences in the muscle response to mechanical overload (51). This may lead to variability in energy expenditure, both at rest and during exercise, as well as a reduction in percent body fat following exercise training between these cohorts (52). However, other factors such as a decline in oxidative capacity and a blunted response to sympathetic activation may need consideration. Previous findings indicate that body composition alone does not fully account for the disparities in energy expenditure between young and older individuals (53).

In the present study, skeletal muscle mass improved in both training groups, but this rise was significantly greater in the E+R compared to R+E group, although the magnitude of the difference was relatively small (E+R: +0.7 kg; R+E: +0.5 kg). Enright et al. found an 8.8% greater increase in quadriceps muscle thickness by using ET before RT during 5 weeks of CT in young soccer players (54). In contrast, other investigations have reported similar increases in skeletal muscle mass and thickness following CT, independent of the exercise order (19, 55). It has been shown that multiple interrelated factors mediate the hypertrophic process and that mechanical tension, muscle damage, metabolic stress, recovery duration and nutrient availability all play a role in exercise-induced muscle growth (56). It appears that the molecular signaling associated with muscle protein synthesis (MPS), specifically the interaction between RT-induced mechanistic target of rapamycin complex 1 (mTORC1) signaling and ET-induced AMPK signaling has important implications for muscular adaptations to exercise (57). mTORC1 is known to play an important role in regulating skeletal muscle mass in response to a wide range of stimuli, including mechanical stress, nutrients, growth factors, and hormones, mainly by regulating translation initiation and MPS (58). In addition, the AMPK signaling cascade has been considered to be a major pathway through which ET may interfere with RT-induced muscle hypertrophy and strength in the context of CT (17). Temporally, the final bout of exercise may dictate the molecular responses of mTORC1 and AMPK signaling and associated crosstalk between them (59). In a rodent study by Ogasawara et al., where ET was performed after RT, inactivation of mTORC1 activity via simultaneous activation of AMPK signaling occurred (60). They reported that MPS and phosphorylation of Ribosomal protein S6 kinase beta-1 (p70S6K), a marker of mTORC1 activity, increased largely when ET was performed prior to RT. Furthermore, increases in the phosphorylation of AMPK were observed only when ET was performed after RT [60]. In humans, Apro et al. reported that elevated AMPK activity does not suppress mTOR mediated signaling when ET is performed prior to RT. They also suggested that, rather than inhibiting mTORC1 signaling, performing ET after RT may instead interfere with the hypertrophic response by increasing expression of key components involved in muscle breakdown (61).

In the current study, upper and lower body power increased in both CT groups. These improvements were greater in the E+R intervention. One possible reason for the difference in the power differences observed between the R+E and E+R may be the greater increase in skeletal muscle mass in the E+R group. Indeed, it has been previously suggested that changes in explosive strength and power mimic changes in skeletal muscle mass (62, 63). However, due to the small magnitude of difference in skeletal muscle mass changes, other explanations, such as the influence of CT order on quality of RT performance and the influence of the aforementioned molecular signaling pathways on adaptations, warrant consideration. Our findings are inconsistent with previous research reporting that exercise order does not influence power improvement following 12 weeks CT in young participants (62). An additional factor that could have influenced the differential adaptations in muscular power is muscular recovery and the associated delayed onset of muscle soreness (DOMS), which may influence power parameters. Previous research has demonstrated significant reductions in power parameters associated with DOMS because of increased intramuscular pressure of edema (64). Notably, ET performed prior to RT has been shown to increase heart rate and blood perfusion which decreases DOMS while facilitating muscle recovery (65). It may be possible that improved muscle perfusion prior to RT in the E+R group ameliorated the adverse effects of DOMS on subsequent exercise performance and resultant power development throughout the study period. Overall, our findings suggest that upper and lower body power improvements are greater when RT is performed following ET during CT in older sarcopenic participants.

The magnitude of increase in VO2max (R+E = ∼9%; E+R = ∼9%) in the present study, without any between-group differences, is similar to previous studies that investigated the effects of CT on elderly cohorts (19, 48, 66). Since VO2max declines by 15% per decade in older individuals (67), the enhancement of 9% in VO2max in both CT groups suggests a reversal of VO2max loss that occurs after ∼6 years. Therefore, incorporating CT in older individuals with sarcopenia could not only serve as an alternative intervention for muscle loss but also for a reduction in cardiovascular risk factors associated with a low cardiorespiratory fitness (68).

The current study has some limitations. Although we did not measure gene expression using biopsy samples, the improvement in circulating SC related markers (expressed during quiescence and proliferation) proposes a potential improvement in SC count and activity following CT in older sarcopenic men. In addition, the elevations in circulating concentrations of signaling molecules increase the likelihood of a receptor interaction, and thus a biological effect within skeletal muscle (69, 70).

Further studies with larger populations investigating molecular aspects in conjunction with biopsy samples can provide information on the precise effect of CT on SC content in sarcopenic cohorts. Additionally, we were not able to assess muscle thickness or cross-sectional area, which would have complemented the total body composition changes reported here. Our participants were older individuals with sarcopenia, and hence we cannot generalize our results to other populations or genders.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: An 8-week CT intervention increased circulating SC related markers, body composition, enhanced muscular power, and VO2max in older sarcopenic participants, regardless of the order of ET and RT. However, performing ET before RT may be more effective at enhancing Myf5 and Pax7, as well as improving both lower and upper body power. While both CT programs produced notable physiological and performance benefits, performing ET before RT during CT may provide the greatest physiological benefits for aging individuals.

sec.title: Conflict of interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: Babak Hooshmand Moghadam has nothing to disclose; Reza Bagheri has nothing to disclose; Damoon Ashtary-Larky has nothing to disclose; Grant M. sec.title: Financial Support
sec.p: This study was supported by Faculty of sports sciences of Tehran University.

sec.title: Ethical standard
sec.p: This study was reviewed and approved by the institutional review board of Tehran University.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The authors wish to thank all the participants in this research project.
